preamble.summary: A Korahite psalm of intense longing for God's dwelling place. The psalmist aches for the temple courts with physical desire — soul and flesh cry out for the living God. Even the sparrow and swallow have found nesting places near God's altars, and the psalmist envies them. The psalm pronounces blessings on those who dwell in God's house, those whose strength is in Him, and those who pass through the Valley of Baca (Weeping) on pilgrimage. It closes with the declaration that a single day in God's courts is better than a thousand elsewhere, and that God withholds no good thing from those who walk with integrity.
preamble.remarkable: This psalm is one of the most emotionally transparent prayers in Scripture. The opening exclamation mah yedidot ('how lovely, how beloved') uses a word from the root yadad ('to love'), making the temple not merely beautiful but loved — an object of affection, not just admiration. The progression from longing (vv. 2-3) to envy of birds (v. 4) to beatitudes about pilgrims (vv. 5-8) to the temple gate (vv. 9-10) to theological declaration (vv. 11-13) maps the journey from desire to arrival to understanding. The psalmist does not merely want to be in the temple; the psalmist wants to be where God is. The temple is beloved because God is there.
preamble.friction: The phrase baqah ('Valley of Baca') in verse 7 is debated. Baca may mean 'weeping' (from bakah, 'to weep'), 'balsam trees' (which grow in arid valleys), or a specific geographic location on the pilgrimage route to Jerusalem. The ambiguity may be intentional: the pilgrims pass through a valley of tears and transform it into a place of springs. The superscription assigns the psalm to the sons of Korah — Levitical musicians who had special access to the temple but may also have experienced periods of exclusion (the Korah rebellion of Numbers 16 cast a long shadow on the family).
preamble.connections: The longing for God's courts echoes Psalm 27:4 ('One thing I have asked of the LORD — to dwell in the house of the LORD all the days of my life'). The bird imagery connects to Psalm 102:7 and Jesus's reference to sparrows in Matthew 10:29-31. The phrase 'sun and shield' in verse 12 is unique in the Psalter — God as sun appears also in Malachi 4:2 ('the sun of righteousness'). The Korahite collection (Psalms 42-49, 84-85, 87-88) consistently explores the theme of longing for God's presence, making this psalm the collection's emotional apex.

verses.text_kjv: To the chief Musician upon Gittith, A Psalm for the sons of Korah.
verses.rendering: For the choirmaster. On the Gittith. Of the sons of Korah. A psalm.
translator_notes: The Gittith also appears in the superscription of Psalms 8 and 81. The sons of Korah were a Levitical guild of temple musicians — descendants of the Korah who rebelled against Moses (Numbers 16) but whose sons were spared (Numbers 26:11). Their survival despite their ancestor's judgment may explain the intense gratitude for access to God's presence that marks their psalms.

verses.text_kjv: How amiable are thy tabernacles, O LORD of hosts!
verses.rendering:
How lovely are Your dwelling places,
O LORD of Hosts!
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm bursts open with an exclamation: mah yedidot mishkenotekha YHWH Tsevaot ('how beloved are Your dwelling places, O LORD of Hosts!'). The word yedidot ('beloved, lovely, dear') comes from yadad ('to love') — the same root as David's name (Yedidyah, 'beloved of the LORD,' 2 Samuel 12:25). The temple is not merely impressive; it is loved. The plural mishkenot ('dwelling places, tabernacles') may refer to the multiple courts and chambers of the temple complex, or it may be a poetic plural intensifying the concept of God's habitation. The divine title YHWH Tsevaot ('LORD of Hosts' — the armies of heaven) appears three times in this psalm (vv. 2, 4, 13), framing the beloved dwelling as the headquarters of cosmic military power. The one whose courts the psalmist loves is also the commander of heaven's armies.
translator_notes: The word mishkan ('dwelling place, tabernacle') is the same word used for the wilderness tabernacle (Exodus 25:9). By using this term rather than bayit ('house') or heikhal ('temple'), the psalmist may be evoking the entire history of God's dwelling among His people — from the portable tent in the desert to the stone temple in Jerusalem.

key_terms.note: yedidot from yadad ('to love') describes not aesthetic beauty but emotional attachment. The temple is yedidot because the psalmist loves it — and loves it because God dwells there. The word transforms architecture into relationship.

key_terms.semantic_range: the LORD of armies, heavenly forces, cosmic powers
key_terms.note: Tsevaot ('hosts, armies') refers to the angelic armies of heaven, the stars as God's celestial army, or the combined forces of heaven and earth under God's command. The title emphasizes God's sovereign power — the one whose house the psalmist loves is not a domestic deity but the commander of all cosmic forces.

verses.text_hebrew: נִכְסְפָ֬ה וְגַם־כָּלְתָ֨ה ׀ נַפְשִׁי֮ לְחַצְר֢וֹת יְהוָ֗ה לִבִּ֥י וּבְשָׂרִ֑י יְ֝רַנְּנ֗וּ אֶ֣ל אֵֽל־חָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: My soul longeth, yea, even fainteth for the courts of the LORD: my heart and my flesh crieth out for the living God.
verses.rendering:
My soul longs — it even faints —
for the courts of the LORD.
My heart and my flesh cry out
to the living God.
verses.expanded_rendering: Two verbs of desperate desire: nikhsefah ('longs, yearns, pines') and gam kaltah ('even wastes away, fails, is consumed'). The soul (nefesh) does not merely want the temple — it is being consumed by the wanting. The verb kalah means to be finished, used up, exhausted; the longing is physically depleting. Then the whole person responds: libbi u-vesari ('my heart and my flesh') — not just the inner self but the body itself — yeranenu ('cry out, shout for joy'). The verb ranan is typically a shout of joy, but here it is a cry of need. Heart and flesh cry out el El chai ('to the living God'). The title El chai is the key: the psalmist longs for the temple because the God who lives there is alive. This is not nostalgia for sacred architecture; it is hunger for a living presence.
translator_notes: The phrase El chai ('living God') appears also in Joshua 3:10, 1 Samuel 17:26, and Psalm 42:3 (another Korahite psalm). The title distinguishes the God of Israel from the dead idols of the nations — this God is alive, active, present, and that aliveness is what the psalmist's body aches for. The physical language (flesh, heart, fainting) insists that longing for God is not merely spiritual but somatic.

verses.text_hebrew: גַּם־צִפּ֨וֹר מָ֪צְאָה בַ֡יִת וּדְר֤וֹר ׀ קֵ֥ן לָהּ֮ אֲשֶׁר־שָׁ֢תָה אֶפְרֹ֫חֶ֥יהָ אֶֽת־מִ֭זְבְּחוֹתֶיךָ יְהוָ֣ה צְבָא֑וֹת מַ֝לְכִּ֗י וֵאלֹהָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Yea, the sparrow hath found an house, and the swallow a nest for herself, where she may lay her young, even thine altars, O LORD of hosts, my King, and my God.
verses.rendering:
Even the sparrow finds a home,
and the swallow a nest for herself
where she lays her young —
beside Your altars, O LORD of Hosts,
my King and my God.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm shifts from human anguish to avian contentment, and the contrast is devastating. The tsippor ('sparrow, small bird') and deror ('swallow, swift') — insignificant creatures — have found what the psalmist desperately wants: a home near God. The sparrow has found bayit ('a house') and the swallow qen ('a nest') for her young, and these are located et mizbechotekha ('beside Your altars'). Birds nest in the crevices of the altar stonework, and the psalmist envies them. They have permanent residency in the temple; the psalmist can only visit. The closing address — YHWH Tsevaot, malki ve-Elohai ('LORD of Hosts, my King and my God') — is deeply personal: the cosmic commander is also my King, my God. The possessive pronouns turn theology into intimacy.
translator_notes: The grammar of this verse is debated. Some read 'beside Your altars' as the location of the nests (birds literally nesting in the altar structure). Others read it as the destination of the psalmist's longing, with the bird imagery as a separate observation. The first reading is more poignant: even the birds have what I cannot have — a permanent place near God's altars.

verses.text_hebrew: אַ֭שְׁרֵי יוֹשְׁבֵ֣י בֵיתֶ֑ךָ ע֝֗וֹד יְֽהַלְל֥וּךָ סֶּֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: Blessed are they that dwell in thy house: they will be still praising thee. Selah.
verses.rendering:
Blessed are those who dwell in Your house;
they are always praising You. Selah.
verses.expanded_rendering: The first of three beatitudes in this psalm: ashrei yoshvei veitekha ('blessed are those who dwell in Your house'). The word ashrei ('blessed, happy, fortunate') describes a state of deep well-being. Those who dwell (yoshvei, from yashav, 'to sit, to remain, to inhabit') in God's house are the Levitical priests and musicians whose daily existence is in the temple precincts. Their entire life is praise: od yehallelukha ('they are continually praising You'). The word od ('still, yet, continually') means the praise never stops — it is their permanent condition, not an occasional activity. The psalmist looks at these temple dwellers and pronounces them blessed because their proximity to God is constant. For someone who aches even to visit, permanent residence is the ultimate joy.
translator_notes: The Levites and priests who served in the temple had residential quarters within the temple complex. Their 'dwelling' was literal, not merely spiritual. The psalmist's beatitude reveals the social dimension of the longing: some people get to live where the psalmist can only pilgrimage.

verses.text_hebrew: אַשְׁרֵ֣י אָ֭דָם עוֹז־ל֥וֹ בָ֑ךְ מְ֝סִלּ֗וֹת בִּלְבָבָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Blessed is the man whose strength is in thee; in whose heart are the ways of them.
verses.rendering:
Blessed is the person whose strength is in You,
in whose heart are the highways to Zion.
verses.expanded_rendering: The second beatitude shifts from those who dwell to those who journey. Ashrei adam oz lo vakh ('blessed is the person whose strength is in You') — the pilgrim's strength does not come from physical stamina but from God. And mesillot bilvavam ('highways in their hearts') — the pilgrim roads to Jerusalem are internalized, mapped onto the heart. The pilgrims carry the road within themselves. The word mesillot ('highways, raised roads, built-up paths') refers to the major routes that pilgrims traveled to reach Jerusalem for the festivals. These roads exist both on the ground and in the pilgrim's desire. The person who has the highway to God's house in their heart is blessed because the journey has already begun inside them.
translator_notes: The word mesillot ('highways') is the same word used in Isaiah 40:3 ('Prepare the highway of the LORD in the wilderness'). The internal highway is the spiritual counterpart of the physical road — both lead to God's presence. This verse influenced later Jewish and Christian interpretations of pilgrimage as both physical journey and spiritual state.

verses.text_hebrew: עֹבְרֵ֤י ׀ בְּעֵ֣מֶק הַ֭בָּכָא מַעְיָ֣ן יְשִׁית֑וּהוּ גַּם־בְּ֝רָכ֗וֹת יַעְטֶ֥ה מוֹרֶֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Who passing through the valley of Baca make it a well; the rain also filleth the pools.
verses.expanded_rendering: The pilgrims pass through emeq ha-Bakha ('the Valley of Baca/Weeping') — an arid, desolate valley on the route to Jerusalem. But the pilgrims transform it: ma'yan yeshituhu ('they make it a spring'). The verb yeshituhu ('they set it, they make it') means the pilgrims' passage through the dry valley turns it into a watered place. Whether this is literal (the rainy season coinciding with pilgrimage) or metaphorical (the pilgrims' faith transforming barrenness), the point is the same: those who journey toward God turn desolation into abundance. And gam berakhot ya'teh moreh ('also the early rain covers it with blessings') — the word moreh is both 'early rain' and 'teacher,' and berakhot is both 'pools' and 'blessings.' The double meanings layer the verse: the rain fills the pools, and the Teacher clothes the journey with blessing.
translator_notes: The Valley of Baca is not identified with certainty. If from bakah ('to weep'), it is a valley of tears that pilgrims must cross. If from baka ('balsam tree'), it is an arid valley where only drought-resistant trees grow. Either reading produces the same theological point: the path to God passes through desolation, and the journey itself transforms the landscape.

key_terms.semantic_range: valley, lowland; weeping, balsam trees, arid place
key_terms.note: The Valley of Baca represents the hardship of the pilgrimage route — the difficult terrain between the pilgrim's starting point and God's dwelling. The theological principle embedded in the image is that the journey to God's presence necessarily passes through suffering, and that suffering is transformed by the journey itself.

verses.text_hebrew: יֵלְכ֣וּ מֵחַ֣יִל אֶל־חָ֑יִל יֵרָאֶ֖ה אֶל־אֱלֹהִ֣ים בְּצִיּֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: They go from strength to strength, every one of them in Zion appeareth before God.
verses.rendering:
They go from strength to strength;
each one appears before God in Zion.
translator_notes: The phrase me-chayil el chayil ('from strength to strength') describes pilgrims who grow stronger as they travel — the opposite of what physical exertion should produce. The nearer they get to God's house, the more energy they have. The verb yera'eh ('appears') is a Niphal form meaning 'is seen' — the pilgrim appears before God, is seen by God. The pilgrimage ends in mutual seeing: the pilgrim sees God's dwelling, and God sees the pilgrim.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהוָ֣ה אֱלֹהִ֣ים צְבָא֑וֹת שִׁמְעָ֥ה תְ֝פִלָּתִ֗י הַאֲזִ֨ינָה ׀ אֱלֹהֵ֖י יַעֲקֹ֣ב סֶֽלָה׃
verses.text_kjv: O LORD God of hosts, hear my prayer: give ear, O God of Jacob. Selah.
verses.rendering:
give ear, O God of Jacob. Selah.
O LORD God of Hosts, hear my prayer;
translator_notes: The address combines three divine titles: YHWH (the personal covenant name), Elohim (the generic divine title), and Tsevaot (Lord of Hosts). The parallel adds Elohei Ya'aqov ('God of Jacob'), grounding the cosmic title in covenant history. The verbs shim'ah ('hear') and ha'azinah ('give ear, listen attentively') are synonymous pleas for divine attention — the psalmist interrupts the meditation on pilgrimage with direct petition.

verses.text_hebrew: מָ֭גִנֵּנוּ רְאֵ֣ה אֱלֹהִ֑ים וְ֝הַבֵּ֗ט פְּנֵ֣י מְשִׁיחֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, O God our shield, and look upon the face of thine anointed.
verses.rendering:
Look upon our shield, O God,
and gaze on the face of Your anointed one.
translator_notes: The word magen ('shield') may refer to God as Israel's shield, or to the king as Israel's shield. The word meshikhekha ('Your anointed one') is mashiach — the anointed king of the Davidic line. The psalmist asks God to look favorably on the king, through whose leadership the nation's worship is sustained. In later interpretation, this verse was read messianically — the anointed one whose face God gazes upon becomes the awaited Messiah.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י ט֥וֹב יוֹם֮ בַּחֲצֵרֶ֢יךָ מֵ֫אָ֥לֶף בָּחַ֗רְתִּי הִ֭סְתּוֹפֵף בְּבֵ֣ית אֱלֹהַ֑י מִ֝דּ֗וּר בְּאׇהֳלֵי־רֶֽשַׁע׃
verses.text_kjv: For a day in thy courts is better than a thousand. I had rather be a doorkeeper in the house of my God, than to dwell in the tents of wickedness.
verses.rendering:
For a single day in Your courts is better than a thousand.
I would rather stand at the threshold of my God's house
than dwell in the tents of wickedness.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm's most famous declaration: tov yom ba-chatserekha me-alef ('a day in Your courts is better than a thousand'). The thousand days are not specified — a thousand days anywhere else, doing anything else. One day near God outweighs nearly three years away from Him. Then the comparison sharpens: bacharti histofef be-veit Elohai mi-dur be-ohalei resha ('I choose to stand at the threshold of my God's house rather than live in the tents of wickedness'). The verb histofef means 'to stand at the threshold, to linger at the doorstep' — not even inside the house but at its entrance. The psalmist would rather be a nobody at God's door than a resident in luxury built on wickedness. The word dur ('to dwell, to live') contrasts with histofef — full residence versus threshold-standing. The psalmist chooses the lesser position near God over the greater position far from Him.
translator_notes: The verb histofef (Hithpolel of suf or safaf) is debated: it may mean 'to stand at the threshold' or 'to be a doorkeeper.' Either way, it describes the lowest possible position of access — the person who barely gets inside, who stands at the outer edge. The psalmist's preference for the margin of God's house over the center of the wicked's tent is the psalm's defining value statement.

key_terms.semantic_range: to stand at the threshold, to be at the doorstep, to serve as doorkeeper
key_terms.note: histofef places the psalmist at the lowest rung of temple access — the very entrance, the outermost edge of sacred space. The word captures the psalm's central theology: any proximity to God, no matter how marginal, surpasses any comfort, no matter how great, that exists apart from God.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י שֶׁ֨מֶשׁ ׀ וּמָגֵן֮ יְהוָ֢ה אֱלֹ֫הִ֥ים חֵ֣ן וְ֭כָבוֹד יִתֵּ֣ן יְהוָ֑ה לֹ֥א יִמְנַע־ט֝֗וֹב לַהֹלְכִ֥ים בְּתָמִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: For the LORD God is a sun and shield: the LORD will give grace and glory: no good thing will he withhold from them that walk uprightly.
verses.rendering:
For the LORD God is a sun and a shield;
the LORD gives grace and glory.
No good thing does He withhold
from those who walk with integrity.
verses.expanded_rendering: The theological foundation of the psalm's longing: ki shemesh u-magen YHWH Elohim ('for the LORD God is a sun and a shield'). This is the only place in the Hebrew Bible where God is explicitly called shemesh ('sun'). God is the source of light, warmth, life, and growth — and simultaneously a magen ('shield'), the source of protection and defense. One metaphor radiates; the other deflects. Together they describe a God who both illuminates and guards. Then the gifts: chen ve-khavod yitten YHWH ('the LORD gives grace and glory') — chen ('grace, favor, attractiveness') is unearned kindness, and kavod ('glory, weight, honor') is the visible manifestation of divine presence. And the sweeping promise: lo yimna tov la-holekhim be-tamim ('He does not withhold good from those who walk in integrity'). The word tamim ('integrity, wholeness, blamelessness') echoes Job 1:1 — those whose walk is whole receive everything good that God has to give.
translator_notes: The sun metaphor for God was risky in ancient Israel, where sun worship was a constant temptation (2 Kings 23:11 records Josiah removing horses dedicated to the sun from the temple). By calling God 'sun,' the psalmist claims for YHWH what the nations attributed to solar deities — light, warmth, life-giving power — while containing it within Israelite monotheism.

key_terms.semantic_range: grace, favor, charm, attractiveness; glory, honor, weight, splendor
key_terms.note: chen ('grace') is undeserved favor — the quality that makes someone look upon you with kindness without you earning it. kavod ('glory') is weight and substance — the visible, tangible manifestation of God's presence and honor. Together they describe what God gives to those who draw near: unearned favor and unmistakable significance.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהוָ֥ה צְבָא֑וֹת אַֽשְׁרֵ֥י אָ֝דָ֗ם בֹּטֵ֥חַ בָּֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: O LORD of hosts, blessed is the man that trusteth in thee.
verses.rendering:
O LORD of Hosts,
blessed is the one who trusts in You.
verses.expanded_rendering: The third and final beatitude closes the psalm with elegant simplicity. YHWH Tsevaot, ashrei adam bote'ach bakh ('LORD of Hosts, blessed is the person who trusts in You'). After the elaborate longing, the envied birds, the pilgrim highways, the threshold standing, the sun and shield — it all reduces to one word: bote'ach ('trusts'). The verb batach means to lean on, to rely upon, to feel secure in. The blessed person is not the one who achieves access to God's presence through effort or status but the one who trusts. Trust is the currency of this psalm — it is what turns longing into blessing, pilgrimage into arrival, and threshold-standing into joy.
translator_notes: The psalm began with YHWH Tsevaot (v. 2) and ends with YHWH Tsevaot (v. 13), forming an inclusio. Everything between is framed by the same name: the LORD of Hosts. The final beatitude strips away every external condition — temple access, pilgrimage, residence — and locates blessedness in trust alone. Anyone who trusts is blessed, whether they stand at the threshold or dwell in the innermost court.
